# Transcrição: 10-Blood Draw

- **Fonte:** 10-Blood Draw
- **Duração:** 06:32
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

I'm walking across a cold parking lot on a Saturday morning, walking towards this awful edifice of a building. It's brown and gray. It looks like poured concrete. It's the kind of brutalist architecture that you see from the 1970s that makes it so clear to me that people in the 70s hated themselves and hated everything about their lives. And I hate this building. I hate it too, not because of the way it looks, but because the thing that happens inside of it. Somewhere up on the third floor at the end of a long hallway, there's a little room. And in that little room right now, there's a person pressing a needle into another person's arm and pulling out blood, a simple blood draw. It's the reason I'm here today. I have a physical coming up and I'm here to get my blood drawn and I'm terrified. I've known about this blood draw for a week and I have not stopped thinking about it for the entire week. Normally when I come to a place like this, my wife is with me and she holds my hand when they tie that hose around your arm and they stick the needle in so it's a little less terrible. But we have a baby now, Clara. She's one. And to wake up your wife on a Saturday morning at seven and ask her to get dressed and then to wake up the baby and get the baby dressed and put the baby in a car seat to come down to this place to do this thing that happens routinely all over the world constantly, I did that. I tried to wake her up and she said no. And that was terrible. But it is not as terrible as what I have to do right now. And so I walk through those doors and I'm in the lobby and I press the button for the elevator and I wait for that carriage of doom to come down. The first time I went for a blood draw with my wife, Alicia, was at Sloan Kettering. One of my students had cancer about 10 years ago and I agreed to donate platelets, which is basically the most courageous thing I have ever done in my entire life. And when I was finished donating, I was sitting in a little room eating a cookie and drinking apple juice alongside Alicia and the nurse came in. And she told my wife, I have been doing this job for 25 years and I have never had a worse patient than your husband. And I was thinking, you've been doing this for 25 years? Like what human being chooses the occupation of sticking needles into other people and sucking out their blood? Like of all the jobs you could choose in this world, that is the one that you chose. Like I know someone is assigned to press the button to launch the nuclear weapons in the event that needs to be done, but I don't want to know that person and I don't want to be that person's friend and I don't want to know these people who are going to stick needles in me and draw out blood. But now I have to go see one of them. And so the elevator doors open and I'm on that long corridor and as I'm walking down towards

## [03:02 - 06:02]

the door, I am thinking about how nurses before now have always told me I need to be tougher. And I hate that because I'm tough. Like I go through to Wenglin winters in hoodies and sneakers and that's it. And we have a toaster oven and my wife bought these wooden tongs to pull the toast out when it's done. I put my whole hand in and I just take that toast right out. I don't give a fuck. I'm tough. But this is something else for me. And so I get into the waiting room and there's no one there so I'm called right into the phlebotomist's office. It's a guy. It's a young guy. And I sit down in the chair and I say to him what I always say to these people before it starts. I say, listen, I'm sorry, but this is going to be really hard for me. I'm really afraid. I'm sorry if I make it difficult for you. And he stops what he's doing. Everything in the room stops. And he sits down in the stool and rolls right up to me and he says to me, why is this hard for you? No one has ever asked me that question before. And so I tell him that when I was 12, I got stung by a bee. I was allergic to the bee, but I didn't know it because in the 1980s nobody was allergic to anything. And so I ended up on my dining room floor on the phone with my mother all alone in my house gasping for a breath that I couldn't find. When the paramedics broke the door down, they found me with no heartbeat and no respiration and they brought me back. But for the next three nights, my body continued to react as if it still had venom in it. And so I would blow up like a balloon and they would inject me every time with epinephrine again and again. And then I had to learn to give myself shots in case I got stung by a bee again and it was before auto-injectors. So I had to actually give myself shots of sterilized water in my arm. I tell him that is why I'm so afraid today. And he puts his hands on my lap and he says, I get it. He says that's called a negative feedback loop. Then he tells me that when he was a boy, very little, a dog bit him in the face and he touches his cheek and there's this tiny white line that you wouldn't see if he didn't point to it. He tells me, I can't go to my friend's house unless they put the dog in the basement. He says it doesn't make any sense. It's irrational fear, but it's real just like yours. He says, I understand. I'll help you. It's the best blood draw I've had in my life. When I get back to my car, I sit behind the wheel and I cry. I can't believe this man that I have found who asked me a question that no one has asked me before and gave me an answer that I've been looking for for so long. And so when I take my students camping in the fall and a little girl tells me that she's afraid of the dark, I say, I understand. It's going to be fine.

## [06:02 - 06:32]

And when my wife tells me she doesn't want the kids on the front lawn playing alone because someone's going to come by and kidnap them and I know that's crazy, I don't say that. I say, I get it, honey. I know what you're saying. And when my daughter Clara, who is allergic to peanuts in the same way I'm allergic to bees, tells me she's afraid to try a new food because it scares her. I tell her, I understand, honey. I understand completely. Thank you.
